coredata.dc:title: Quaternized salicylaldehyde Schiff base modified mesoporous silica for efficiently sensing Cu(II) ions and their removal from aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuanyuan
Cao, Xiang
Wu, Guohao
Wang, Jie
Zhang, Tianlin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, quaternized salicylaldehyde Schiff base modified SBA-15 mesoporous silica (SBA-AP) was developed. The characterization of SBA-AP shows that the Schiff base ligand has been successfully introduced and ordered mesoporous structure of SBA-15 has been well-preserved after the post-grafting procedure. The quaternized linkage made the as-prepared nanohybrid very dispersible in water, and the salicylaldehyde Schiff base acted as a multifunctional ligand for complexing Cu(II). By adding Cu(II) into the SBA-AP suspension, the absorbance UV intensity at ∼ 420 nm gradually increased. In addition, the adding Cu(II) caused dramatic fluorescence quenching that was very selective for Cu(II) ions. The linear detection range for the Cu(II) chemosensor was 0.05–4 mg/L, and the detection limit was 3.7 × 10−7 M. The SBA-AP could also be used as an adsorbent for removing Cu((II) from water. The adsorption kinetics were fast and followed the pseudo-second-order kinetics model. The adsorption isotherms were fitted well by Langmuir isotherm mode and theoretical maximum Langmuir adsorption capacity of the SBA-AP toward Cu(II) was 34.73 mg/g. Thus, the SBA-AP offers promise for detecting, adsorbing, and recovering Cu(II) ions from water.

head.ce:title: Quaternized salicylaldehyde Schiff base modified mesoporous silica for efficiently sensing Cu(II) ions and their removal from aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials Function Control Technology, School of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang 222005, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials Function Control Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials Function Control Technology, School of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang 222005, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, quaternized salicylaldehyde Schiff base modified SBA-15 mesoporous silica (SBA-AP) was developed. The characterization of SBA-AP shows that the Schiff base ligand has been successfully introduced and ordered mesoporous structure of SBA-15 has been well-preserved after the post-grafting procedure. The quaternized linkage made the as-prepared nanohybrid very dispersible in water, and the salicylaldehyde Schiff base acted as a multifunctional ligand for complexing Cu(II). By adding Cu(II) into the SBA-AP suspension, the absorbance UV intensity at ∼ 420 nm gradually increased. In addition, the adding Cu(II) caused dramatic fluorescence quenching that was very selective for Cu(II) ions. The linear detection range for the Cu(II) chemosensor was 0.05–4 mg/L, and the detection limit was 3.7 × 10−7 M. The SBA-AP could also be used as an adsorbent for removing Cu((II) from water. The adsorption kinetics were fast and followed the pseudo-second-order kinetics model. The adsorption isotherms were fitted well by Langmuir isotherm mode and theoretical maximum Langmuir adsorption capacity of the SBA-AP toward Cu(II) was 34.73 mg/g. Thus, the SBA-AP offers promise for detecting, adsorbing, and recovering Cu(II) ions from water.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (Cu(II)) is an essential trace element for humans and is involved in various enzymes that play vital roles in important metabolic processes and physiological functions [1,2]. Unfortunately, electroplating, chemical and mineral industry plants have discharged large amounts of Cu(II) directly into natural water. Cu(II) is toxic at high concentrations and poses serious risks to humans and the environment due to its bio-accumulable and non-biodegradable nature [3]. Thus, Cu(II) at excessive concentrations is classed as a priority pollutant by the US Environmental Protection Agency [4,5]. By now, Cu(II) concentrations in water are generally determined by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry or atomic absorption spectroscopy [6]. However, the complex natures of the analytical methods and the costs of the instruments severely restrict Cu(II) analysis. Thus, extensive efforts have been devoted to the preparation of various small-molecule fluorescent probes for detecting Cu(II) which are very sensitive, selective, cheap, and easy to use [7,8].

A hybrid heterogeneous sensor can be prepared by incorporating a small-molecule probe into an inorganic solid support. There is much interest in such hybrid heterogeneous sensors because they can be used to eliminate heavy metal ions from solution and can easily be regenerated after use [9,10]. Mesoporous silica is an ideal support in hybrid sensors because it is stable and has a large surface area, uniform channels, and tunable pore sizes [11,12]. Silica also absorbs and emits little light in the visible spectrum, making it useful in methods involving fluorescence excitation and emission measurements [13]. In addition, the regularly arranged channel structure of mesoporous silica enables the hybrid fluorescent chemosensor to improve its fluorescence intensity [14], which was ascribed to aggregation emission enhancement.

Fluorescent chromophores can easily be covalently bonded to a silica matrix, and this prevents undesirable leakage of the chromophores during use. Fluorescent hybrid mesoporous silica materials have been prepared through co-condensation and grafting reactions [15,16]. Co-condensation involves a single synthetic step, and the functional groups tend to be evenly distributed throughout the product structure. However, the large amounts of the organosilicate involved in the reaction may result in the reduction of order of the resulting material. Mesoporous channel surfaces have many silanol groups, meaning various functional groups can be immobilized on the surfaces using a silane treatment [17]. Post-synthetic grafting offers the great advantage that the mesostructure of the material is maintained well.

Many fluorescent molecule functionalized mesoporous silica materials have been prepared and used as chemosensors for various anions in aqueous media [18–23]. It has been reported that Schiff base derivatives with large π-conjugated systems are useful for detecting ions due to their chromogenic groups [24,25]. Schiff base could be easily prepared through condensation of primary amine groups with active carbonyl group-containing compounds, forming the imine linkages containing nitrogen and oxygen atoms for ion coordination [26].

Recently, various fluorescence nanosensors based on Schiff base modified mesoporous silica have been developed [27–30]. For instance, Zou et al. covalently coupled a bis-Schiff base onto SBA-15 mesoporous silica to give a fluorescent chemosensor that was very selective for Fe(III) and Hg(II) [31,32]. 2-Hydroxy-naphthyl has been grafted onto mesoporous silica through Schiff condensation to give a product suitable for detecting and separating Al(III) [33]. Chen et al. grafted 4-chloro-2-[(propylimino) methyl]-phenol on the surface of mesoporous silica membrane via Schiff condensation [14], and the hybrid nonosenser was explored to detect Cu(II) together with other metal ions. Branched Schiff base functionalized mesoporous silica has also been designed and prepared for the recognition, quantification, and extraction of Zn(II) [34]. However, since most fluorescent chromophores attached to mesoporous substrates that have been described are very hydrophobic, the resulting nanosensors may have poor aqueous dispersibilities, which restricts their sensing application in pure water. Precipitation of a nanosensor during the sensing process will give inaccurate quantitative results [35]. It is therefore important to develop hybrid mesoporous chemosensors that can disperse well in aqueous solutions for detecting ions reliably [36].

The aim of this study was to develop a quaternized salicylaldehyde Schiff base modified esoporous silica fluorescent sensor (SBA-AP) with excellent aqueous dispersibility for selectively detecting, adsorbing, and recovering Cu(II) ions from aqueous solutions. Connecting the proper functional organic ligands to the mesoporous siliceous matrix was the key to achieve this goal. As shown in Scheme 1 , the sensor was fabricated by immobilizing the salicylaldehyde Schiff base onto the SBA-15 mesoporous silica surfaces through quaternized linkages. The quaternized linkage was expected to be a strongly hydrophilic group that would allow the nanohybrid to disperse well in aqueous solutions. The terminal salicylaldehyde Schiff base with a large conjugated electron system acted as a probe by selectively complexing aqueous Cu(II) and undergoing marked changes in the optical signal. The product was systematically characterized, and the selective ability of the SBA-AP to detect Cu(II) in water through a change in fluorescence was evaluated. The adsorption kinetics and isotherms were investigated, and the reusability of the nanohybrid for adsorption was also assessed.

Tetraethoxyorthosilicate (TEOS), N,N-diethyl-3-(trimethoxysilyl)-1-propanamine (DATMS), CuSO4·5H2O and 2-aminophenol were obtained from Sinopharm (China) and directly used. All the conventional solvents were analytically pure and obtained from Sinopharm (China). Pluronic P123 (EO20PO70EO20) and anhydrous toluene was provided by Sigma-Aldrich.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of samples were recorded with the standard KBr disks on a Shimadzu Prestige-21 spectrophotometer. The UV–vis absorption was performed on Shimadzu-3700 spectrophotometer. The morphology and shapes of the nanosensor were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) Zeiss Merlin and transmission electron microscope (TEM) Thermo Talos F200X. The low-angle X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Bruker AXS D-8 (Cu-Kα radiation, 2θ range 0.4–5°). Element mappings of samples are acquired using an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) attached to a High angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) Titan G2 60–300. A flame atomic absorption spectrophotometer (FAAS) Beijing Purkinje General Instrument, TAS-990 was employed to determine the concentrations of metal ions in an air-acetylene flame. Malvern Zetasizer Nano was employed to evaluate the Zeta-potential values at 25 °C. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (10 °C/min ramp up to 800 °C) was performed using a TGA analyzer TA Instrument GA Q500 under air atmosphere. Porous structure parameters were obtained by a sorptometer Beijing Beishide H-2000PM1 using the Brunauer − Emmett − Teller (BET) and Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method through nitrogen adsorption–desorption. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum was carried out on Bruker BIOSPIN AV400 nuclear magnetic resonance instrument with chloroform (CDCl3) as a solvent. Element analyses were determined by a LECO TC400.

5-Chloromethylsalicylaldehyde (5-CSA) was synthesized exactly according to procedures reported in literature [37]. 1H NMR (CDCl3, 500 MHz, δ ppm): 11.06 (1H, s, Ar-OH), 9.89 (1H, s, CHO), 7.58 (1H, s, Ar–H), 7.54–7.56 (1H, dd, Ar–H), 7.00–6.98 (1H, d, Ar–H), 4.58 (2H, s, Ar–CH2Cl). The spectrum is shown in Fig. S1.

First, mesoporous silica SBA-15 was synthesized using sol–gel method exactly according to the literature reported using TEOS as the silica precursor and Pluronic P123 copolymer as the structure directing template [38,39]. To remove the template packed inside the mesopore channels, the silica material was calcinated in air at 540 °C for 4.5 h with a slow heating rate (1 °C/min). Afterwards, 4.0 g of the pristine SBA-15 and 4.0 mL of DATMS were added in 100 mL of anhydrous toluene. The reaction was performed under reflux for 12 h under nitrogen atmosphere. Subsequently, the resultant product SBA-DATMS was recovered through centrifugation and then washed with methanol. Finally, the product was dried under vacuum.

SBA-CSA was prepared through heterogeneous quaternization reaction. Briefly, 4.0 g of SBA-DATMS was dispersed in 50 mL of acetonitrile. Then the suspension was slowly added dropwise into the 50 mL of acetonitrile solution containing 4.0 g of 5-CSA which was stirred in an ice bath. The mixture was stirred at the room temperature under nitrogen atmosphere. After 48 h of reaction, the pale white product SBA-CSA was recovered through centrifugation and then washed with methanol. Finally, the product was dried under vacuum.

The nanosensor was prepared via heterogeneous Schiff condensation. Basically, 3.8 g of SBA-CSA was suspended in 80 mL of absolute ethanol, and 50 mL of ethanol solution containing 4.0 g of 2-aminophenol was added into the SBA-CSA suspension. After stirring at room temperature for 36 h, the yellow product SBA-AP was recovered through centrifugation and then washed with methanol. Finally, the product was dried under vacuum.

The fluorescence response of SBA-AP toward various metal ions was assessed. Initially, the stock suspension of SBA-AP (0.5 mg/L) was prepared. Then 2.0 mL of metal ion aqueous solution was added to 2.0 mL of the stock suspension and the suspension was mixed thoroughly. Finally, the suspension was transferred to a quartz cuvette to record the UV–vis absorption spectrum or the fluorescence emission spectrum at an excitation wavelength of 369 nm.

For the quantification of Cu(II) ion, 2.0 mL of Cu(II) solution of different concentrations (0 to 8 mg/L) was added to 2.0 mL stock suspension of SBA-AP (0.5 mg/L) and the suspension was mixed thoroughly. Finally, the suspension was transferred to a quartz cuvette to record the fluorescence emission spectrum at an excitation wavelength of 369 nm. The calibration curve was plotted to calculate the linear range and regression equation.

Adsorption behaviors of SBA-AP to remove Cu(II) were evaluated from batch experiments in 100 mL glass conical flasks. Deionized water was directly used in the adsorption experiments to avoid the additional pH adjustment. Briefly 0.04 g of SBA-AP was dispersed in a water solution containing Cu(II) at various concentrations and the suspension was placed in an automatic shaker under 150 rpm shaking at 25 °C. After adsorption, SBA-AP was removed by filtration with syringe filter and residue concentration of Cu(II) in the filtrate was measured by FAAS. The amount of adsorbed ion Q (mg/g) was determined according to the following equation [40]:
(1)
Q = (
C 0

) V / m

where

is the Cu(II) ion concentration (mg/L) prior to experiments,

The quaternized salicylaldehyde Schiff base ligand was grafted onto SBA-15 surfaces using a three-step post-modification process. The surfaces of SBA-15 substrate were first modified by adding N,N-(diethyl)propyl groups using a silylation treatment. The SBA-15 with tertiary amine groups grafted on was then reacted with 5-CSA by performing a quaternization reaction. The salicylaldehyde Schiff base containing an imine unit was then formed through Schiff condensation with aminophenol.

The success of each synthetic step was initially confirmed by FTIR spectra. As depicted in Fig. 1 , typical Si–O–Si vibration bands were found at 1081, 804, and 460 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra, indicating that all the samples had condensed silica networks. Characteristic peaks were found at 1468 and 1390 cm−1 in the SBA-DATMS spectrum but not the pristine SBA-15 spectrum and these new peaks were attributed to bending vibrations of C–H bonds in the silane coupling agent. Broad bands at around 2973 and 2939 cm−1 were assigned to C–H bond stretching vibrations. Quaternization with 5-CSA caused a new peak to occur in the SBA-CSA spectrum at around 1659 cm−1, which was due to C = O vibrations in aldehyde groups that had been introduced. Reacting the SBA-CSA with aminophenol caused the peak at 1659 cm−1 to disappear and a new sharp peak to appear at 1642 cm−1. The new peak was assigned to C = N stretching vibrations in the imine linkages that had formed. The results indicated that the salicylaldehyde Schiff base ligand had been successfully grafted.

XPS spectra was used to identify changes in the surface element composition caused by each modification step. As shown in Fig. 2 , the X-ray photoelectron spectra indicated that the main elements in the pristine SBA-15 were O and Si. The silane treatment by DATMS caused C 1 s and N 1 s peaks to appear in the SBA-DATMS spectrum, indicating that carbon and nitrogen atoms had been added to the surfaces. This confirmed the successful functionalization of SBA-DATMS with secondary amine groups. Reacting the SBA-DATMS with 5-CSA caused weak Cl 2 p and Cl 2 s peaks to appear, implying that quaternized salicylaldehyde containing chloride anions had been immobilized on the surfaces of material. Ultimately, the N 1 s peak was markedly stronger in the SBA-AP spectrum than the SBA-CAS spectrum, indicating that salicylaldehyde Schiff bases were successfully formed on the surfaces after Schiff condensation.

TGA was performed to investigate the thermal stabilities of the hybrid materials and the thermal degradation processes that occurred. As shown in Fig. 3 , the pristine SBA-15, SBA-DATMS, SBA-CSA, and SBA-AP had lost 1.18%, 15.04%, 24.78%, and 31.13% of their initial masses, respectively, when the temperature reached 800 °C. The thermogravimetric analysis plot for the pristine SBA-15 indicated that negligible mass was lost at 800 °C, indicating that the calcination process had completely removed organic components and physically absorbed water. The percentage of the mass that was lost by heating increased as the nanohybrids became more modified through the reaction steps, confirming that organic components were attached to the material during each modification step. The thermal changes in all of the samples indicated that the quaternized Schiff base ligand had been successfully introduced to the hybrid material.

Elemental analysis was used to evaluate the elemental composition of samples. The nitrogen contents of pristine SBA-15, SBA-DATMS, SBA-CAS, and SBA-AP were determined to be 0, 1.15, 1.03 and 1.82 mmol/g, respectively. Based on the changes of nitrogen contents, it is estimated that the amount of N,N-(diethyl)propyl group grafted on silica and iminophenol group in Schiff base were 1.15 and 0.79 mmol/g, respectively. The mass percentage of introduced groups can be calculated by the following equation: ω = q·Mw × 100%, where q (mol/g) is the content of introduced agent, Mw (g/mol) is molecular weight of the introduced group. According to the equation, the loadings of the two introduced agents were determined to be 13.2% and 8.4%, respectively. Besides, the difference in weight loss between the SBA-15 and SBA-DATMS was about 13.8%, while the difference between SBA-CSA and SBA-AP was 6.4%. Therefore, it can be concluded that the loadings of N,N-(diethyl)propyl group and iminophenol groups evaluated by elemental analysis are comparable with the values quantified by TGA.

The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms for the mesoporous samples are shown in Fig. 4 . A characteristic type IV IUPAC standard isotherm with H1 hysteresis loop was found at relative pressures (P/P0) of 0.5–0.8 for each sample, indicating that the hybrid materials had well-developed ordered mesoporous structures. Table 1 summarizes the textural parameters of the materials obtained from the isotherm data. In each functionalization step, the pore volumes and specific surface area of the materials decreased continuously. This would have been caused by organic components being introduced and the channels becoming partly occupied. As shown in Fig. S2, each sample had a narrow pore size distribution and the pore sizes decreased as the modification process proceeded. The specific surface area and pore volume for the SBA-AP nanosensor were 125 m2/g, 0.28 mL/g, and average pore size was 4.3 nm. The well-preserved ordered pore structure of SBA-AP would allow the free diffusion of the target ions to occur within the channels.

The pristine SBA-15 and SBA-AP were characterized by low-angle XRD. As shown in Fig. S3, each sample gave one strong and two weak well-resolved characteristic diffraction peaks. The strong peak was indexed to the characteristic (1 0 0) diffraction line, and the weak peaks were indexed to the characteristic (1 1 0), and (2 0 0) diffraction lines. This confirmed that well-arranged hexagonal mesopore structures were present. After grafting the salicylaldehyde Schiff base groups onto the surfaces of the silica substrate, the relative intensities of characteristic diffraction peaks decreased a bit due to the slightly decreasing long-range order in the channels by filling some of the pores.

SEM and TEM were used to characterize the morphologies, shapes, and sizes of the hybrid SBA-AP, and the images are exhibited in Fig. 5 a–c. As shown in the SEM image, SBA-AP had a characteristic rod-like morphology with mean particle diameters of 350–550 nm. The TEM images illustrated that the SBA-AP had many well-ordered mesopore channels and the array of honeycomb structure, indicating the mesopore structure ordering of the SBA-15 host was preserved well though multi-step modification process. The distributions of the constituent elements in SBA-AP with Cu(II) adsorbed were examined by HAADF-STEM and EDS element mapping. As shown in Fig. 5d, the mesoporous structure can be clearly observed in the HAADF-STEM image. In the elemental maps for the hybrid material, the colors for Si, O, N, and Cu were uniformly dispersed and the adsorbed Cu(II) was found in similar areas to the other elements. This confirmed that Cu(II) was adsorbed from the solution by the grafted ligands and homogeneously distributed within the inner channels of the mesoporous silica.

So far, the reported Schiff base ligands for constructing the hybrid mesoporous nanosensor were high hydrophobic, resulting in the poor aqueous dispersibility of the resulting nanosensers. Good aqueous dispersibility is a crucial feature of a nanosensor that can serve to accurately determine the concentration of a target ion in water [41]. The grafted quaternized linkage was strongly hydrophilic and would have helped the SBA-AP disperse in an aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. S4, a large amount of pristine SBA-15 substrate precipitated when it had been stored over 30 min. However, the SBA-AP nanosensor still remained well-dispersed in water within the same storage time. Besides, the wettability of pristine SBA-15 and SBA-AP were evaluated using water contact angle measurement. As exhibited in Fig. S5, the water contact angles were measured to be 33.5° for pristine SBA-15 and 23.4° for SBA-AP, indicating that wettability of the modified membranes had been further improved by the surface modification with quaternized ligand. Therefore, the fluorescent sensing study of SBA-AP was conducted in the aqueous solution without any organic co-solvent.

After adjusting the pH values of the suspension with hydrochloric acid or sodium hydroxide solution, the apparent zeta-potentials for SBA-AP at different pH values were determined. The zeta-potentials are shown in Fig. 6 a, from which it can be seen that the SBA-AP zeta-potentials continually decreased as the pH increased. The isoelectric point of SBA-AP was determined to be at pH 8.21. When the pH value was below 8.21, the nanosensor was positively charged because of protonation of the Schiff base ligand. This is very important because conformational differences in the Schiff base ligand at different pH values would affect the fluorescence of the nanosensor.

Due to the absence of chromophore groups, the pristine SBA-15 should provide no fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 6b, the obvious luminance phenomena of quaternized salicylaldehyde Schiff base grafted on SBA-AP can be detected because of the large electron conjugate system with C = N electron flowing bridge. Fluorescence spectra of SBA-AP in aqueous solutions with different pH conditions were acquired. The intensity of the fluorescence peak emitted by SBA-AP excited at 369 nm increased as the pH increased in the range pH 2.0–8.0. The pH-dependent increase in fluorescence intensity was caused by deprotonation of the Schiff base ligand, which will have become more likely as the pH increased from 4.0 to 8.0. Maximum fluorescence intensity was found at pH 8.0, which was close to the pH at the isoelectric point. However, the fluorescence peak intensity decreased significantly at pH value higher than 8.0. The intensity of the fluorescence peak at pH 9.0 was only about ∼ 50% of the intensity at pH 8.0. In this case, the obvious fluorescence intensity decline may be due to the ionization of phenolic hydroxyl groups at pH 9.0. Meanwhile, the SBA-AP fluorescence peak maximum was at ∼ 540 nm in the pH range 2.0–5.0, but the maximum was blue-shifts to 490 nm at pH 7.0 and higher. The fluorescence emission band at 490 nm may have been caused by the deprotonated form of the salicylaldehyde Schiff base and the band at 540 nm by the protonated form. We conclude that the fluorescence properties, including the emission intensity and wavelength, of SBA-AP in an aqueous solution were affected by the pH, suggesting that aqueous solutions at constant pH were required for the reliable and accurate detection of ions. Thus, the following fluorescence sensing experiments were performed in NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4 buffer solution at the optimum pH of 7.0.

SBA-AP was used as an ionic chemosensor to sense cations and sensing ability was firstly investigated by studying the effects of Cu(II) ions on the UV–vis absorption properties of the SBA-AP. UV–vis absorption spectra of the SBA-AP were recorded in the presence of Cu(II) at various concentrations in aqueous solutions. The spectra are shown in Fig. 7 . The intensity of the peak at ∼ 400 nm increased slowly as the Cu(II) concentration increased, indicating that Cu(II) interacted with the grafted salicylaldehyde Schiff base. However, since the changes of UV–vis absorption spectra were insignificant variable, no further research on UV–vis colorimetric analysis was conducted for quantitative determinations.

The sensing capability of SBA-AP was investigated by addition of various concentrations of Cu(II) ions ranging 0–4 mg/L. Fluorescence emission spectra of the SBA-AP in the presence of Cu(II) are shown in Fig. 8 a. SBA-AP excited at 369 nm in the absence of Cu(II) gave strong fluorescence emission at 490 nm at room temperature. Upon the addition of Cu(II) ions into the suspension, the fluorescence emission intensity of the SBA-AP monotonously decreased as the Cu(II) concentration increased from 0.05 to 4 mg/L, but no obvious change and shift appeared in the shape of maximum fluorescence emission. The change of fluorescence intensity was obvious and could be easily identified by the naked eyes (Fig. S6). We conclude that the SBA-AP could be used as a ratiometric fluorescent chemosensor for detecting Cu(II) ions.

Fluorescence quenching usually originates from static and/or dynamic quenching, and each mechanism can be described using a different quenching equation [42]. In general, the dynamic quenching and static quenching processes can be analyzed by Stern-Volmer equation and Lineweaver-Burk equation, respectively, which are shown as follows [43]:
(2)

where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of Cu(II), respectively, [Q] is the Cu(II) concentration, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and KD is the Lineweaver-Burk constant.

Initially, the fluorescence quenching was analyzed by Stern-Volmer equation. As shown in Fig. S7, the plot of F0/F for SBA-AP versus [Cu(II)] ranging from 0.05 to 4 mg/L was linear. However, in some cases, Stern-Volmer equation is applicable to not just dynamic quenching, but the static quenching [44-46]. Thus, the fitting of Stern-Volmer equation alone was insufficient to accurately determine the quenching mechanism occurred [46].

The Lineweaver–Burk plot of (F0 − F)−1 against [Cu(II)]−1 is shown in Fig. 8b. The relationship was also linear over the Cu(II) concentration range 0.05–4 mg/L, and the correlation coefficient R2 was 0.9949. KD, determined from the slope and intercept of the line, was 1.25. Since the decrease in SBA-AP fluorescence as the Cu(II) concentration increased was described well by the Lineweaver–Burk equation, it was possible that static quenching may play a major role in the interaction of SBA-AP with Cu(II) [32].

To further confirm the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence quenching at different temperatures were studied. Since dynamic quenching depends upon diffusion, the bimolecular quenching constants are expected to increase upon the increasing temperature [47]. In contrast, lower values of the static quenching constants would be observed due to the decrease of complex stability at higher temperature. As shown in Fig. S8, Stern–Volmer curves of SBA-AP with [Cu(II)] at different temperatures showed the slope of the curve decreased with the increase in the temperature, confirming that the quenching process was static [44,48].

This static quenching mechanism would indicate that the fluorescent molecule and a metal ion interact to form a nonfluorescent complex [32]. The fluorescent molecule in the ground state would have been involved in the interaction, and the quenching efficiency would have been controlled by the metal ion concentration and the complex formation constant. The salicylaldehyde Schiff base had multiple coordination sites and a large π-electron system, so the ligands on the SBA-AP surfaces would have strongly interacted with Cu(II) to form non-fluorescent coordination complexes.

By replicating the detections of 0.05 mg/L Cu(II) 11 times with SBA-AP suspension at a concentration of 0.25 mg/L [49], the detection limit (DL) of the SBA-AP was determined to be 0.024 mg/L (3.7 × 10−7 M) at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which implies that the SBA-AP is potentially used for detection of Cu(II) ions in the low concentration. The detection limit of the nanohybrid was compared with other fluorescence nanosensors reported in the literature (Table 2 ). It is observed that the although detection limit of the SBA-AP for Cu(II) is higher than those of some works, such as naphthalimide modified SBA-15 [20], and anthracene modified mesoporous silica [50], it is lower than or close to the those of other nanosensors [14,51-53]. On the whole, SBA-AP prepared in this study presents excellent sensitivity toward the Cu(II) detection.

The color change of SBA-AP suspension treated with different metal ions was studied. The SBA-AP suspension alone was yellow–brown. When the SBA-AP suspension was added with Cu(II) ions, the color quickly turned to bright yellow (Fig. S9). The SBA-AP suspension changed to other colors when other metal ions were present. Therefore, the unique color of the Cu(II) treated SBA-AP could be used to determine whether Cu(II) ions were present in an aqueous solution.

Selectivity is an important feature of a fluorescence sensor. In the presence of various common metal ions each at a concentration of 10 mg/L, only Cu(II) markedly decreased fluorescence emission from the SBA-AP following excitation at 369 nm (Fig. 9 a), while almost no obvious changes of fluorescence emission from the SBA-AP were observed upon the addition of interfering ions including Ni(II), Mn(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Cr(III), Mg(II), K(I), Na(I), Co(II), Zn(II) and Hg(II). In addition, the competition behaviors were also evaluated by comparing the change in the fluorescence response of SBA-AP for Cu(II) ion in the presence of the above interfering metal ions and the results are shown in Fig. 9b. It can be seen that the background ions interfered little (or not at all) with the ability of the SBA-AP to detect Cu(II). Thus, hybrid material SBA-AP shows a remarkable selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Cu(II) over other metal ions.

It is known that solution pH could affect the adsorption properties of adsorbents. The final pH value of the suspension is dependent on the initial solution pH. The final suspension pH was determined to be around 6.8 after adsorption of Cu(II). Considering the neutral deionized water was used as solvent, no obvious pH change occurred during the adsorption process, which was in line with the literature [54].

The adsorption capacities of the SBA-AP toward Cu(II) at different contact times were determined in order to evaluate its potential as a practical absorbent material. At an initial Cu(II) concentration of 100 mg/L, the amount of Cu(II) adsorbed increased quickly during the initial contact time (Fig. 10 ). This could be attributed to the large numbers of available ligands for chelation and the high level of free Cu(II) ions at this point. After 60 min, the adsorption rate decreased and eventually reached a plateau. Equilibrium would have been reached because of the adsorption sites becoming saturated. An adsorption time of 240 min was used in subsequent experiments. The result shows that SBA-AP has rapid adsorption kinetics as compared with other SBA-15 type adsorbents [55].

The mechanism controlling the adsorption process was investigated by using linear form pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second order kinetic models, which are generally described as the following equations [56,57]

As exhibited in Table S1, the calculated theoretical value of Qe from the pseudo-second-order model was in line with the final adsorbed amount value. Besides, the value of R2 of pseudo-second-order model was close to 1, indicating that the adsorption kinetics of Cu(II) by SBA-AP was better represented by pseudo-second-order model. Therefore, the adsorption was fast at the initial stage of adsorption and the adsorption rate of Cu(II) onto SBA-PA might be controlled by chemical process via exchange or sharing of electrons [58].

The adsorption capacities of the SBA-AP for Cu(II) at various initial Cu(II) concentrations were determined. As exhibited in Fig. 11 , the amount of adsorbed Cu(II) at equilibrium increased as the initial Cu(II) concentration increased. At a high initial Cu(II) concentration, the adsorption curve tended to reach a plateau because the adsorption sites could become saturated. The adsorption isotherms were fitted using the conventional used Langmuir and Freundlich models, described using the following equations [59]:

where Qm and Qe are the theoretical maximum sorption capacity (mg/g) and equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg/g), respectively. Ce is the Cu(II) concentration at equilibrium (mg/L), KL is the Langmuir isotherm constant (L/mg), KF is the Freundlich parameter (mg/g) and n is the Freundlich heterogeneity factor. The adsorption capacity is plotted as a function of the initial Cu(II) concentration in Fig. 11, and the curves fitted using the Langmuir and Freundlich models are shown in the inset of Fig. 11 and Fig. S11. The experimental data were described well by the Langmuir sorption isotherm with the correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9996 (Table S2), which was consistent with the sorption isotherm of other functionalized SBA-15 for adsorption [60]. This result reveals adsorption sites on the surface of the SBA-AP were homogenously distributed and monolayer sorption occurred through a complexation mechanism [61]. The isotherm study also indicated that the theoretical maximum Langmuir adsorption capacity of SBA-AP toward Cu(II) was 34.73 mg/g. This result corresponds closely to the adsorption study of Cu(II) bysalicylaldehyde or pyridylaldehyde Schiff base modified SBA-15 type silica [62,63], implying that the quaternized linkage has no adverse effects on the adsorption performance of this new type of nanostructured materials.

The adsorption experiment was conducted repeatedly to evaluate the potential for the SBA-AP to be used for the removal of Cu(II) multiple times. By treating the SBA-AP with Cu(II) adsorbed with complexing agents of 0.05 M EDTA for 1 h, the SBA-AP was regenerated. The results are presented in Fig. 12 . It can be observed that the adsorption capacity of the SBA-AP decreased only slightly after each adsorption–regeneration cycle and was > 85% of the original adsorption capacity after four cycles. This suggests that the SBA-AP has good reusability and is promising for removing Cu(II) from wastewater.

By using quaternized salicylaldehyde Schiff base as the binding ligand, we have synthesized a new multifunctional fluorescent nanosensor SBA-AP. The characterization and analysis results indicated that the ligand was successfully immobilized on the mesoporous silica surfaces using a post-synthetic grafting method and that the ordered mesopore structure of SBA-15 substrate was well preserved. The resultant material had excellent aqueous dispersibility, which was beneficial for sensing applications in water. The hybrid material changed color and fluorescence characteristics when Cu(II) ions bounded to it. The nanosensor fluorescence was quenched when exposed to Cu(II) ions, and the quenching data were described well by the Lineweaver–Burk equation. SBA-AP fluorescence quenching was selective for Cu(II). Besides, the Cu(II) adsorption kinetics were fast and were described well by a pseudo-second order kinetics model. The adsorption also followed Langmuir monolayer isotherm, with maximum theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of 34.73 mg/g. Thus, SBA-AP has great potential for detecting and removing Cu(II) ions from aqueous solutions.
